Why we rewrote our build system

After three years of escalating Makefile pain, we finally bit the bullet and migrated the whole engineering organisation onto a proper task graph runner. Here is what we learned, what we shipped, and what we would do differently.

The pain we were running from

Our old build pipeline was a 4,000-line Makefile with implicit rules layered on top of explicit ones. Onboarding a new engineer took three weeks. A typo in one rule could silently rebuild every artefact. We could not parallelise across machines because the dependency graph was not introspectable.

What we shipped

We picked a content-addressed task graph runner with first-class remote caching. The migration took six engineers four months — about half the time we estimated, mostly because we wrote a small DSL that translated existing Make targets into the new format.

What surprised us

The biggest unexpected win was that the new system surfaced flaky tests we did not know we had. The old Makefile retried silently; the new runner failed loudly. We fixed eighty-seven flakes in the first month.

What we would change

We would migrate test targets first, application targets second, and infrastructure targets last. We did the opposite, and infrastructure became a long tail that blocked the cutover for two months.

Written by the Initech Engineering team — © 2026 Initech.
